title.en: Ibn Battuta: Thirty Years on the Road
title.ar: ابن بطوطة: ثلاثون عامًا على الطريق

subtitle.en: A 75,000-mile journey across the medieval Islamic world
subtitle.ar: رحلة ٧٥٬٠٠٠ ميل عبر العالم الإسلامي في العصور الوسطى

description.en: Follow the Tangier-born scholar Ibn Battuta on the longest documented journey of the pre-modern world — from a 1325 departure for Hajj that grew into three decades of travel through Egypt, Syria, the Swahili coast, India, China, and Mali.

narration.en: Aged 21, Ibn Battuta sets out alone for the Hajj — never imagining he will not see his family again for nearly 30 years.

title.en: 2. Cairo: the largest city in the world

narration.en: Mamluk Cairo astonishes him: 12,000 water-carriers, 30,000 muleteers, and madrasas everywhere.
He studies with Damascene shaykhs through Ramadan, then joins the great Hajj caravan that will take him south to Mecca.
He performs Hajj for the first of seven times across his career and resolves to keep traveling.
Down the East African coast — Mogadishu, Mombasa, Kilwa — he calls Kilwa one of the most beautiful and best-built cities in the world.

title.en: 6. Eight years as a qadi in Delhi

narration.en: Sultan Muhammad bin Tughluq appoints him chief qadi of Delhi on a salary of 12,000 silver dinars — and later sends him to China as ambassador.
He reaches the great Chinese port of Quanzhou (Zaytun) — the easternmost point of his journey — and finds a thriving community of Muslim merchants.
Crossing the Sahara, he reaches the court of Mansa Sulayman in Mali — and stops at Timbuktu before turning home.
After ~75,000 miles and three decades, he returns home — and dictates the Rihla to Ibn Juzayy at the order of the Marinid sultan.

text.en: "My departure from Tangier — my birthplace — took place on Thursday 2nd Rajab 725 (June 14, 1325) ... I was at that time twenty-two years of age, with the intention of making the pilgrimage to the Holy House and visiting the tomb of the Prophet ﷺ."

His Indian sojourn under Muhammad bin Tughluq lasted eight years — by far the longest stay of his career. He served as a chief qadi, married into the court, and barely survived several brushes with the sultan's volatile favor.

Quanzhou — Zaytun in his Arabic — was then the greatest port in the medieval world. He noted four large Friday mosques, a flourishing community of foreign Muslims, and ships sailing as far as Borneo, Java, and the Maldives.

Modern scholarship treats some legs of the Rihla — the Volga–Bulgar excursion, parts of the China narrative — as compressed or borrowed; but the broad outline is corroborated by independent sources, and the achievement is unprecedented.

body.en: The Rihla is the most extensive single source on the social, religious, and political life of the 14th-century Islamic world. Ibn Battuta's eyewitness accounts of Mamluk Cairo, Tughluq Delhi, the Maldives sultanate, and Mansa Musa's Mali are foundational to the historical record.

note.en: Itinerary follows the standard scholarly chronology (Gibb 1958, Dunn 1986, Hrbek 1962). Some leg-by-leg dates are reconstructions and remain debated — flagged where significant. Pending scholar review before promotion to 'verified'.
